1: The river of the water of life flowing from the throne — the primary OT template is Ezekiel 47:1–12, where the man with the measuring rod shows Ezekiel a river flowing from the threshold of the eschatological temple, growing deeper as it flows eastward, bringing life wherever it goes ("everything will live where the river goes," Revelation 22:9), with fruit trees on both banks whose leaves do not wither and whose fruit does not fail. The second template is Genesis 2:10–14 (the river flowing from Eden branching into four rivers). Revelation's river combines both: the eschatological temple-river (Ezekiel) and the primordial garden-river (Genesis), because the new creation restores Eden and fulfills the temple simultaneously. "Flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb" grounds the source of life not in a created structure but in the co-sovereignty of the Father and Son.
2: The tree of life on each side of the river bearing twelve fruits (one per month) with leaves for the healing of nations echoes Ezekiel 47:12 directly ("On the banks, on both sides of the river, there will grow all kinds of trees for food. Their leaves will not wither, nor their fruit fail, but they will bear fresh fruit every month, because the water for them flows from the sanctuary. Their fruit will be for food and their leaves for healing"). The tree of life itself echoes Genesis 2:9; Revelation 3:22–24, where access was blocked when the cherubim were placed at Eden's gate. Access now restored = the exile from Eden reversed. "Leaves for the healing of the nations" extends the tree's benefit beyond Israel to universal scope — the Gentile nations included in the eschatological restoration, just as they are included in the great multitude (Revelation 7:9) and the city (Revelation 21:24, 26).
3: "No longer will there be any curse" (katathema) — the reversal of Genesis 3:17–19, where the ground was cursed because of Adam's sin ("cursed is the ground because of you; in pain you shall eat of it all the days of your life"). The removal of the curse is the completion of creation's redemption: not just human beings but the created order itself is healed (Romans 8:19–22: "the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay"). "His servants will serve him" (latreuousin autō) — the verb latreuō carries priestly-service connotations (service in the sanctuary); all the redeemed serve as priests before God, fulfilling Exodus 19:6 ("a kingdom of priests and a holy nation") in its fullest eschatological extension.
4: "They will see his face" — direct vision of the divine face was the supreme eschatological hope and a present impossibility. Exodus 33:20 ("you cannot see my face, for man shall not see me and live") established the barrier. The Aaronic blessing ("the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you; the LORD turn his face toward you and give you peace," Numbers 6:24–26) was the liturgical proxy for direct vision not yet available. Psalms 17:15 ("As for me, I shall behold your face in righteousness; when I awake, I shall be satisfied with your likeness") expresses the eschatological hope. Matthew 5:8 ("blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God") anticipates the beatific vision. In the new Jerusalem, the barrier is permanently dissolved. The divine name on the foreheads (cf. Revelation 14:1; Revelation 7:3) echoes the high priest's forehead-plate engraved with YHWH's name (Exodus 28:36–38) — all the redeemed now bear what only the high priest formerly wore.
5: "There will be no more night. They will not need the light of a lamp or the light of the sun, for the Lord God will shine upon them" — Isaiah 60:19–20 fulfilled (cf. Revelation 21:23, 25). The created luminaries and artificial lamps are both superseded. "They will reign forever and ever" (basileusousin eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn) — the redeemed's eternal co-reign with God fulfills Daniel 7:27 ("the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven shall be given to the people of the saints of the Most High; their kingdom shall be an everlasting kingdom") and the promise to the victors throughout Revelation 2–3 (Revelation 2:26–27; Revelation 3:21). Ruling is participation in divine governance — the new creation is not static but dynamically ordered by those who share the throne.
6: "The Lord, the God who inspires the prophets" (ho kyrios ho theos tōn pneumatōn tōn prophētōn, "the God of the spirits of the prophets") — God as the source of prophetic inspiration echoes Numbers 11:29 ("Would that all the LORD's people were prophets, that the LORD would put his Spirit on them"), Zechariah 1:6, and 1 Corinthians 14:32 ("the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets"). The angel's statement grounds the authority of Revelation's prophecy in the same divine Spirit who inspired the OT prophets — Revelation is not a new kind of revelation but the culmination of the same prophetic tradition. "These words are trustworthy and true" repeats the divine authentication of 21:5 and Revelation 19:9.
7: "Behold, I am coming soon" (idou erchomai tachy) — the first of three appearances of this declaration in Revelation 22 (Revelation 22:7, 12, 20). The imminence formula does not necessarily mean calendar-imminence but eschatological urgency: the End has been inaugurated with Christ's death and resurrection, and the final coming could occur at any moment. "Blessed is the one who keeps the words of the prophecy of this scroll" is the sixth of Revelation's seven beatitudes (Revelation 1:3; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 16:15; Revelation 19:9; Revelation 20:6; Revelation 22:7; Revelation 22:14). The beatitude structure runs throughout the book, each blessing a particular response to the revelation — this one blesses faithful hearing and obedience to the entire prophetic message.
8: John's prostration before the angel echoes the identical scene in Revelation 19:10, where the same refusal is given. The repetition is not accidental — it brackets the New Jerusalem vision (Revelation 21–22) with the same anti-angel-worship correction that appears before it (Revelation 19:10), emphasizing that the glorious things John has seen do not transfer their glory to the angelic messenger. The double occurrence reinforces the danger of angel-worship as a live temptation in the early church, consistent with Colossians 2:18 ("let no one disqualify you, insisting on worship of angels") and the broader trend in Second Temple Judaism where angelology sometimes approached what might be called angelic quasi-worship.
9: "I am a fellow servant with you and with your brothers the prophets and with all who keep the words of this scroll — worship God" — the angel's refusal of worship and self-identification as a fellow servant places angelic and prophetic ministry on the same level of service to the one God. "Worship God" (tō theō proskyněson) is the corrective throughout Revelation — where the beast-system demands worship (Revelation 13:8, 12, 15), where false religion demands worship of intermediaries, the entire prophetic message redirects to the one God. The angel's equality with the prophets positions Revelation's angelic mediation within, not above, the community of those who serve God.
10: "Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near" — the direct contrast with Daniel 12:4 ("But you, Daniel, close the words and seal the book, until the time of the end") is intentional and explicit. Daniel was told to seal his prophecy because its fulfillment was distant; John is told not to seal his because "the time is near" (ho kairos engys estin). Revelation uses this distinction to claim that it belongs to the final epoch of prophetic revelation: what Daniel sealed as future-oriented, Revelation unseals as present-in-fulfillment. The unsealed book is for immediate congregational use — to be read aloud in the churches (Revelation 1:3), not preserved for later scholarly decoding.
11: "Let the evildoer still do evil, and the filthy still be filthy, and the righteous still do right, and the holy still be holy" — the formula of irreversibility echoes Ezekiel 3:27 ("let the one who will hear, hear; and let the one who will refuse, refuse") and Daniel 12:10 ("the wicked shall act wickedly. And none of the wicked shall understand, but those who are wise shall understand"). The eschatological urgency creates a paradox: the book is unsealed and its invitation remains open (Revelation 22:17: "let the one who is thirsty come"), yet the coming judgment means that character is being fixed in the direction it is already moving. The tension is the tension of the already/not-yet: the judgment is imminent, the opportunity for response is now, and delay courts finality.
12: "My reward is with me, and I will give to each person according to what they have done" — Isaiah 40:10 ("Behold, the Lord GOD comes with might, and his arm rules for him; behold, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him") and Isaiah 62:11 ("Say to the daughter of Zion, 'Behold, your salvation comes; behold, his reward is with him'"). The divine judge bringing recompense is one of the central OT eschatological images. "According to what they have done" (kata to ergon autou) is the judgment-by-works formula from Psalms 62:12; Proverbs 24:12; Jeremiah 17:10; Matthew 16:27; Romans 2:6; 2 Corinthians 5:10. This does not contradict grace but describes the evidence by which genuine faith is manifested — works are the fruit, not the root, of the life that inherits the new creation.
13: "I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End" — the threefold self-identification formula accumulates three pairs: Alpha-Omega (used of the Father in Revelation 1:8; applied to Christ here), First-Last (used of Christ in Revelation 1:17; Revelation 2:8; applied to YHWH in Isaiah 44:6; Revelation 48:12), and Beginning-End (used of the Father in Revelation 21:6). The convergence of all three formulas in one declaration signals the theological unity of the Father and Son: what is said of the Father in 1:8 and 21:6 is now said identically of Christ. The divine comprehension of all time — from the first letter to the last, from the beginning point to the end point — is claimed by the returning Lord who authored and will complete the entire story.
14: "Blessed are those who wash their robes, that they may have the right to the tree of life and may go through the gates into the city" — the seventh and final beatitude of Revelation (cf. Revelation 1:3; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 16:15; Revelation 19:9; Revelation 20:6; Revelation 22:7). Washing robes in the Lamb's blood (Revelation 7:14) = cleansed by the atoning sacrifice; the white robe = righteousness. Access to the tree of life directly reverses Genesis 3:22–24 (where access was blocked by the cherubim after the fall). Going through the gates echoes Psalms 118:20 ("This is the gate of the LORD; the righteous shall enter through it") — the liturgical entry-gate tradition of Israel's worship enacted at the level of the eternal city. The two privileges (tree of life + city entrance) are the supreme goods of Revelation 21–22 summarized in one beatitude.
15: "Outside are the dogs, those who practice sorcery, the sexually immoral, the murderers, the idolaters, and everyone who loves and practices falsehood" — the six excluded categories parallel the eight-item catalog of Revelation 21:8, with "dogs" leading. In Deuteronomy 23:17–18 (LXX), "dog" refers to male cult prostitutes excluded from Israel's assembly; in Philippians 3:2 ("beware of the dogs... beware of those who mutilate the flesh") Paul uses it for those who corrupt the gospel. The term carried both cultic-impurity and moral-disgrace connotations. "Everyone who loves and practices falsehood" closes the list with the satanic root-sin (Revelation 12:9; Revelation 20:10: the devil is "the deceiver") — all the excluded categories ultimately express either the love of death or the love of falsehood, the definitional marks of the domain opposed to the God of life and truth.
16: "I, Jesus, have sent my angel to give you this testimony for the churches. I am the Root and the Offspring of David, and the bright morning star" — Jesus's personal authentication of the revelation echoes Revelation 1:1 (the chain: God → Christ → angel → John → churches). "The Root and Offspring of David" combines two Davidic claims: Isaiah 11:10 ("the root of Jesse") and the Davidic-offspring promise; "offspring" connects to the branch/sprout imagery of Isaiah 11:1; Jeremiah 23:5; Zechariah 3:8. Jesus is simultaneously David's ancestor-Lord (Mark 12:35–37: "How can the scribes say that the Christ is the son of David? David himself... calls him Lord") and David's descendant. "The bright morning star" echoes Numbers 24:17 ("a star shall come out of Jacob") and Revelation 2:28 (the promise to the victors: "I will give him the morning star"). The title is self-referential: Christ gives to the victors what he himself is.
17: "The Spirit and the Bride say, 'Come!'" — the eschatological invitation issued jointly by the Holy Spirit and the church (the Bride). The double "Come!" has two directions: (1) upward, addressed to the returning Christ ("Come, Lord Jesus!" of Revelation 22:20 — the Maranatha prayer); (2) outward, addressed to the thirsty ("let the one who is thirsty come"). This bidirectional invitation is the church's defining eschatological posture: simultaneously praying for the parousia and extending the gospel invitation to the world. "Let the one who is thirsty come — let the one who desires take the water of life without cost" quotes Isaiah 55:1 ("Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters... come, buy wine and milk without money and without price") — the Isaianic free-offer of the eschatological feast is now the church's standing invitation.
18: "If anyone adds anything to them, God will add to that person the plagues described in this scroll" — the warning against addition echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 ("You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it") and Deuteronomy 12:32 ("Everything that I command you, you shall be careful to do. You shall not add to it or take from it"). The application of the Deuteronomic Torah-formula to Revelation positions the book as having the binding authority of divinely given Torah — not replacing Scripture but completing it. The penalty for addition uses the book's own content (the plagues of Revelation) as the punishment — the Word is self-enforcing. This formula also appeared in ancient treaty documents as a curse against those who alter treaty texts, giving the warning both scriptural and cultural resonance for the first-century audience.
19: "If anyone takes words away from this scroll of prophecy, God will take away from that person any share in the tree of life and in the holy city" — the removal penalty (losing access to the tree of life and the city) is the reversal of the book's climactic promises: the tree of life (Revelation 22:2, 14) and the holy city (Revelation 21:2, 10) are the ultimate goods Revelation describes. To tamper with the book is to forfeit the goods it promises. The warning continues the Deuteronomic formula of Revelation 22:18 — adding earns the plagues, subtracting loses the blessings. The symmetry of the paired warnings (add/subtract; earn plagues/lose blessings) reflects the covenantal structure of Deuteronomy 27–28 (curses for disobedience, blessings for obedience) applied to the prophetic text itself.
20: "Yes, I am coming soon. Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!" — the closing exchange between the risen Christ and the community contains the Aramaic liturgical formula Maranatha, preserved in 1 Corinthians 16:22 ("If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed. Maranatha!") and the Didache 10.6 ("Let grace come and this world pass away. Hosanna to the God of David. If anyone is holy, let him come; if anyone is not so, let him repent. Maranatha. Amen"). The Aramaic origin (the vernacular of early Palestinian Christianity) proves this was a pre-Pauline liturgical prayer used at the Lord's Supper — the earliest Christian communities were already praying for the Lord's return in the context of the eucharistic meal. Revelation ends by placing itself within that living liturgical tradition: the book is read aloud at worship and the congregation responds with the ancient prayer for the parousia.
21: "The grace of the Lord Jesus be with God's people. Amen" — the standard Pauline letter-closing benediction (Romans 16:20; 1 Corinthians 16:23; 2 Corinthians 13:14; Galatians 6:18; Ephesians 6:24; Philippians 4:23; 1 Thessalonians 5:28; 2 Thessalonians 3:18; Titus 3:15; Phlm 25) applied to Revelation confirms that the book belongs to the letter-genre as well as the apocalyptic-prophetic genre (cf. Revelation 1:4–6: the letter opening to the seven churches). Grace (charis) is the defining gift of the new covenant — the unmerited divine favor that the book has chronicled from the throne-room to the new Jerusalem — and it is the last word before the "Amen." The revelation that began with grace "from him who is and who was and who is to come" (Revelation 1:4) ends with the grace of the Lord Jesus: both the triune source and the incarnate mediator of grace are named; the community that receives it is named "God's people" — the covenant relationship that was the thread of all Scripture is the final word.
